title: Pricing as Energy, Not Strategy
subtitle: A reading on what underpricing reveals about the emotional layer underneath your rates
excerpt: A reading on the difference between a pricing strategy and a pricing wobble. Covers why most underpricing happens below the level of strategy, what the wobble is doing, and what changes when the emotional layer shifts rather than just the number.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain psychology and business coaching literature on pricing psychology, undercharging, and self-worth in entrepreneurship.

practice.whenToUse: Read when the gap between your current prices and your wanted prices has been running for a while, or when the strategy advice makes sense but the number keeps not going up.

content.text: A reading on the layer underneath pricing decisions. Most people who underprice already know they underprice. The problem is not information. It is what happens in the body the moment they consider the number that is actually warranted.
The wobble is not a strategy problem

There is no shortage of pricing advice. Calculate your costs. Research the market. Anchor to a higher number. Position clearly. Most people who chronically underprice have read the advice and understand it. They lower the number anyway — not because the strategy was unclear but because something below the strategy level overrides it at the last moment.

That something is usually a fear: of being seen as greedy, of being rejected, of overestimating their own worth. Sometimes it is a belief that a lower price signals generosity or humility. Sometimes it is just the physical sensation of discomfort that arrives when a full number is considered, and the relief of lowering it.

The pricing wobble is doing something useful in its own terms: managing the fear of rejection and the discomfort of being seen to value your work highly. Lowering the price before anyone objects removes the risk of the explicit no. It also removes the income, but that feels secondary to the emotional management.

For many women, the wobble is also connected to older material: the belief that wanting more is unbecoming, that wealth draws negative attention, that asking for the full amount is the same as being difficult. The price becomes a proxy for how much space you're willing to take up.
What changes when the energy shifts

When the emotional charge reduces, the strategy advice becomes actionable. Not because the advice changed — because the resistance that was overriding it is no longer there at full force. The number can be written down and said aloud without the body immediately contracting around it.

The tapping sequence, the energy statement, and the journal prompts in this library work on the charge directly. The aim is not to transform you into a confident pricer. It is to reduce the noise so the number that was already there can be said.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain psychology and business coaching literature on pricing psychology, undercharging, and self-worth in entrepreneurship.
